perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon 30 days prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon 10 days prior written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days could be problematic in Michigan, potentially conflicting with reasonable notice requirements under contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL 440.2309 addresses the termination of contracts, implying that reasonable notification must be provided. Reducing the notification period to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the specifics of the agreement and industry standards, leading to legal challenges regarding proper termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 440.2309 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 440.2309 Download Section Chapter 440 Act 174 of 1962 174-1962-2 174-1962-2-3 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE (EXCERPT) Act 174 of 1962 440.2309 Time of performance; contract termination. Sec. 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. History: 1962, Act 174, Eff. Jan. 1, 1964
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 10 days, which introduces a potential contradiction with Michigan law regarding reasonable notification as described in the law explanation and perturbation explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically MCL 440.2309(3), states that termination requires "reasonable notification." By significantly reducing the notice period, the modified contract risks violating this requirement, thus creating a legal contradiction. The original text established a definitive 30-day period, while the changed version introduces a shorter period that may not be considered reasonable under the law, increasing the risk of legal challenges.

perturbation.original_text: Disclosing Party may serve written request on Recipient for return or destruction of its Confidential Information at any time up to six (6) months after the termination or expiry of this Agreement and Recipient shall, within thirty (30) days of such request or termination, return to the Disclosing Party (or its designees) or certify as destroyed all Confidential Information, in whatever form, including written or electronically recorded information and al l copies thereof (other than copies retained in automatic back -up and archive systems), provided however that Recipient shall be entitled to retain one copy of the Confidential Information with its legal counsel or other appropriate corporate representativ e to evidence the exchange of information hereunder and in connection with legal or statutory requirements .
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosing Party may serve written request on Recipient for return or destruction of its Confidential Information at any time up to one (1) month after the termination or expiry of this Agreement and Recipient shall, within sixty (60) days of such request or termination, return to the Disclosing Party (or its designees) or certify as destroyed all Confidential Information, in whatever form, including written or electronically recorded information and al l copies thereof (other than copies retained in automatic back -up and archive systems), provided however that Recipient shall be entitled to retain one copy of the Confidential Information with its legal counsel or other appropriate corporate representativ e to evidence the exchange of information hereunder and in connection with legal or statutory requirements .
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe for requesting the return of confidential information to 'one (1) month' after termination might be deemed unreasonably short under certain trade secret laws, especially if the confidential information has a longer lifespan or the industry has typical practices that allow for more extended periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 protects trade secrets. Reducing the request window to one month may be seen as failing to implement reasonable measures to protect trade secrets depending on the nature of the information and standard industry practice, increasing the risk of misappropriation during an unprotected period shortly after contract termination.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the timeframe for requesting the return of confidential information from six months to one month after the termination or expiry of the agreement. The Law Explanation states this change could be seen as failing to implement reasonable measures to protect trade secrets under 18 U.S.C. § 1836, particularly if the confidential information has a longer lifespan. While the snippets do not directly define 'reasonable measures,' the Law Explanation's interpretation of 18 U.S.C. § 1836, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, suggests that reducing the request window may not provide adequate protection, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby unconditionally and irrevocably agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts of the State of Michigan.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby unconditionally and irrevocably agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction to the courts of the People's Republic of China would be problematic if one of the parties is located in the U.S., particularly since the contract is between parties in North America. This may violate fundamental fairness and due process under U.S. law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Fifth Amendment guarantees due process of law. Mandating exclusive jurisdiction in China for a contract primarily executed within North America may deny a U.S.-based party a fair opportunity to present their case, potentially violating due process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fifth Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Fifth Amendment The Fifth Amendment creates a number of rights relevant to both criminal and civil legal proceedings. In criminal cases , the Fifth Amendment guarantees the right to a grand jury , forbids “ double jeopardy ,” and protects against self-incrimination . It also requires that “ due process of law ” be part of any proceeding that denies a citizen “life, liberty or property” and requires the government to compensate citizens when it takes private property for public use. Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment V No person shall be held to answer for a capital, or otherwise infamous crime, unless on a presentment or indictment of a grand jury, except in cases arising in the land or naval forces, or in the militia, when in actual service in time of war or public danger; nor shall any person be subject for the same offense to be twice put in jeopardy of life or limb; nor shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself, nor be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law; nor shall private property be taken for public use, without just compensation. Wex Resources Fifth Amendment Criminal Law / Criminal Procedure Due Process Substantive Due Process Miranda Warning Indictment Privilege Against Self-Incrimination Self-Incrimination Grand Jury Jury Double Jeopardy Collateral Estoppel Immunity From Prosecution Eminent Domain Takings Territorial Jurisdiction Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Fourth Amendment up Sixth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text designates the courts of the People's Republic of China as the exclusive jurisdiction, replacing the original jurisdiction of the state and federal courts of Michigan. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that this change might violate fundamental fairness and due process, especially if one party is in the U.S. The Law Explanation supports this by citing the Fifth Amendment's guarantee of due process, arguing that mandating jurisdiction in China for a contract executed in North America could deny a U.S.-based party a fair hearing. Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces this by stating that the Fifth Amendment ensures 'due process of law' before depriving a citizen of 'life, liberty, or property.' This change significantly undermines due process protections by potentially forcing a U.S. party to litigate in a foreign jurisdiction with potentially different legal standards, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices shall be in writing and delivered by electronic mail, registered or certified mail (return receipt requested) to the appropriate address set forth in the signature block below.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices shall be delivered verbally, without any requirement of written confirmation, to the appropriate contact person as designated by each party. The parties agree to waive any requirement for written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only verbal notice and waiving written confirmation contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which in many jurisdictions (including Michigan) requires certain contracts or modifications to be in writing.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 566.132 dictates that certain agreements are void unless in writing and signed by the party to be charged. If this NDA modifies a prior agreement that falls under the Statute of Frauds, then eliminating written notice would render the notice provision unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 566.132 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 566.132 Download Section Chapter 566 R.S. of 1846 R-S-1846-566-131-81 Previous Section Next Section Revised Statutes of 1846 (EXCERPT) Chapter 81. Of Fraudulent Conveyances And Contracts, Relative To Goods, Chattels, And Things In Action. 566.132 Agreements, contracts, or promises required to be in writing and signed; enforcement; "financial institution" defined. Sec. 2. (1) In the following cases an agreement, contract, or promise is void unless that agreement, contract, or promise, or a note or memorandum of the agreement, contract, or promise, is in writing and signed with an authorized signature by the party to be charged with the agreement, contract, or promise: (a) An agreement that, by its terms, is not to be performed within 1 year from the making of the agreement. (b) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or misdoings of another person. (c) An agreement, promise, or undertaking made upon consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry. (d) A special promise made by a personal representative to answer damages out of his or her own estate. (e) An agreement, promise, or contract to pay a commission for or upon the sale of an interest in real estate. (f) An assignment of things in action, whether intended as a transfer for sale, for security, or otherwise. (g) An agreement, promise, contract, or warranty of cure relating to medical care or treatment. This subdivision does not affect the right to sue for malpractice or negligence. (2) A person shall not bring an action against a financial institution to enforce any of the following promises or commitments of the financial institution unless the promise or commitment is in writing and signed with an authorized signature by the financial institution: (a) A promise or commitment to lend money, grant or extend credit, or make any other financial accommodation. (b) A promise or commitment to renew, extend, modify, or permit a delay in repayment or performa
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification requirements from written to verbal, explicitly waiving written confirmation. The LLM explanation and perturbation explanation both point out that this may contradict the Statute of Frauds in Michigan (MCL § 566.132) if the agreement falls under its purview. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL § 566.132, states that certain agreements are void unless in writing and signed. Therefore, eliminating the requirement for written notice directly contradicts the statute if the underlying agreement is one that falls under the statute's requirements. The change directly opposes the requirement for written agreements, creating a significant legal contradiction.
